author: @lisakingscott7729
text:
I may be mistaken, but I think this isn't really welding, it's brazing or soldering. The 'welding' rods are generally a zinc alloy which melts at a lower temperature than aluminium. But zinc is good at alloying with aluminium, so they make a good bond. However zinc is harder, so there can be issues in major structural applications.
They are really great. I find the best way to use these is to tin both parts first. Use a blow torch to heat the part and a stainless steel wire brush. Melt the rod using the heat of the aluminium and brush in with the wire brush. The brush breaks the oxide layer on the aluminium and you should get a nice tinned surface which is easy to make a joint to.

author: @Kiyarose3999
text: Heating the metal with no distortion is an excellent idea, cos you got it uniformly heated and to a temp that meant not much extra heat was needed. But although for your purposes, the joint should be strong enough. It isn’t actually Welding but sticking the metal together with a bead of Ali either side. Welding involves melting the rod into a pool of molten metal to get proper penetration, which couldn’t be done with that blow Torch you used. As the flame is too big, so you couldn’t get the heat close enough in one small spot to create the pool that the rod needs to be  dipped  into.!. I did my apprenticeship as a sheet metal worker/welder, well my workshop was called the ‘Tinsmiths Shop’, we used to Oxy Acetylene weld Aluminium from 22 gauge up to whole lorry engine blocks. When someone had a cracked Ali Engine block we would have 2- people heating the block up while a third welded it, cos Aluminium transfers heat so well.

replies.author: @wibblywobblyidiotvision
replies.text: Aluminium brazing rhe rods aren't aluminium, but mostly zinc.  It's a braze rather than a weld, but there is (as in brazing ferrous materials with bronze) some material transfer; you end up with a very small layer of zinc / alu alloy between the zinc and the underlying metal.  That's why you can't simply peel the stuff off if it's done properly (as you would be able to with, for example, solder).  It's not welding per se, but it's definitely brazing.

author: @got2kittys
text: You can weld cast iron pieces, big ones, by heating the whole casting. Even an ordinary flux core welder will do well, if your parts are heated.

replies.author: @nil5221
replies.text: @gotnokittys I'm really interested in your comment. Could you give details of the welding please? Arc welder (i.e. stick welder?) Did you mean flux coated, not flux core? And could you give examples of suitable rods?

replies.author: @got2kittys
replies.text:
@nil5221 what I meant was an ordinary wire-feed welder. Even the no gasses, flux core works. Mig welding has a sheilding gas such as argon. 
   In a non shop situation outdoors I have done the heating in a wood fire. Whole machinery manifolds, cast iron covers, engine manifolds. 
  Just get them dull red, all over. Then weld. Alternate heat and welding till finished. Cool slowly.
  Edit: most basic welding rods will work, also.

author: @chuckthebull
text: This is that low heat "soldering" rod,,it's kinda like aluminium brazing in a sense or soldering. welding is melting both mediums together. This is wetting in one to another like solder,  but I have used it with good effect for some structural small jobs. and yes pre heat is essential for this to work well, and pre heat even for higher heat aluminum tig welding that I have also done makes it easier..but it's something people should be aware of that aluminium tig welding rod is a different alloy. I could see some people might get the wrong type and struggle with this method...

I used to just start it and preheat with a torch, but your idea keeps it hot the entire thing,, and it went fast by the looks of it so great job and lesson.

author: @HarleyPebley
text: Does MAPP gas work better than propane?

replies.author: @nunyabidniz2868
replies.text: Heats hotter, but propane is already hot enough to melt aluminum, so save the MAPP for heating thicker sections [1/4" +] or jobs where higher temps are required. [Silver soldering steel, etc.]

replies.author: @HarleyPebley
replies.text: Yep, I know it's hotter, that's why I wondered if in this application, where he has a large mass acting as a heat sink, if it'd work better than propane. Just as an alternative to using the hot plate to pre-heat the entire thing.

author: @Telectronics
text: Did you also have problems like melting the aluminium you wanted to braze ? Mine became liquid like the welding rod :)

replies.author: @nunyabidniz2868
replies.text: Go over to Project Farm channel; he reviewed a bunch of the aluminum solders and several of them are particularly poor, having a eutectic point that's almost identical w/ aluminum's melting point. The good ones melt at a lower temp [surprise surprise!] ;-)

replies.author: @Telectronics
replies.text: If I remember correct mine has around 650°C. I trusted it because it´s a german known brand. And it makes good bonds but I will look at other brands too. Makes life a lot easier when there are solders with lower melting point. I bought mine from a hardware store so internet is your friend I guess.

replies.author: @nil5221
replies.text: I used "Trade flame"  brand on aluminium castings. On the pack it has specs...supposedly many metals in the rod including Titanium and Beryllium (0.0008% which I don't believe). Silicone 6-12 % (a very wide range considering the Beryllium fake accuracy...  but no zinc??  Says melt point 574 to 632 C which also seems a very wide range. The whole thing is quite sus. Anyway the casting melted before the rod. Total rubbish.

replies.author: @Telectronics
replies.text: Mine is from rothenberger rolot604 brand and it´s the only one available in the hardwarestore. Mixture is AlSi which has a higher melting point. It was 550°C before and they changed it to 650°C because they changed the mixture. If you work very fast then it works but overshooting is easy because sometimes it takes a while to flow and not make beads on the material. After melting the base aluminium it weakens and you can toss it in the garbage it gets too brittle then as I find.
